history.text: During this invasion, ten German destroyers, each carrying 200 mountain infantry soldiers, were sent to Narvik.
Narvik offered a port which is ice-free thanks to the warm Gulf Stream, and is naturally large, allowing boats of virtually any size to anchor, up to 208 metres (682 ft) long and 27.
In 1939, Germany's war industry depended upon iron ore mined in Kiruna and Malmberget in Sweden.
Winston Churchill realized that the control of Narvik meant stopping most German imports of iron ore during the winter of 1940.

history.label: Modern city
history.text: The town of Narvik was developed as an all-year ice free port for the Swedish iron mines in Kiruna and Gällivare .

history.label: Civic role
history.text: Obtaining iron ore from Kiruna had one significant problem in that there was no suitable Swedish port.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: The Swedish company (Gällivarre Aktiebolag) built the Iron Ore Line (Malmbanan) to Riksgränsen on the Norway–Sweden border.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: The Norwegian Ofotbanen railway line connects Narvik to the Swedish border.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: It is an important employer and landowner in Narvik, although its influence is not as prominent as in the past. World War II The port of Narvik proved to be strategically valuable in the.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: During the summer season, this ore could be sent by cargo ship to Germany through the Baltic Sea via the Swedish port of Luleå on the Gulf of Bothnia.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: However, when the Gulf of Bothnia froze during the winter, more shipments of the ore needed to be transported through Narvik and, from there, down the west coast of Norway to Germany.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: Equally as important, later in the war, German submarines and warships based there threatened the allied supply line to the Soviet Union.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: This is also considered the first Allied infantry victory in World War II.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: Possession of the Ofotfjord was also important to the German Kriegsmarine (navy) since it provided a refuge for warships like the "pocket battleship" Lützow and the battleship Tirpitz outside the range (at the time).
